items.passage:
Climate proxies are natural records that preserve evidence of temperatures from times before instruments existed. The earliest efforts to reconstruct past climate relied on written human accounts, such as harvest dates and reports of frozen rivers. [■] These sources were vivid but patchy, covering only a few centuries and a handful of regions. They could hint at warm or cold spells, yet they offered no precise numerical measure of temperature.

Later, scientists turned to tree rings, whose annual width reflects growing conditions in a given year. [■] This method extended the record back several thousand years, but it remained limited to forested zones and was easily disturbed by drought or disease. [■] Such gaps left long stretches of the deep past entirely unmeasured, frustrating attempts to chart ancient climate.

The modern breakthrough came with ice cores drilled from polar sheets, where trapped air bubbles store samples of ancient atmospheres. [■] By analyzing the chemistry of these bubbles, researchers can estimate temperatures stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. However, even ice cores face objections, since drilling sites are scarce and the deepest layers are often compressed beyond clear reading.
paragraphs: Climate proxies are natural records that preserve evidence of temperatures from times before instruments existed. The earliest efforts to reconstruct past climate relied on written human accounts, such as harvest dates and reports of frozen rivers. [■] These sources were vivid but patchy, covering only a few centuries and a handful of regions. They could hint at warm or cold spells, yet they offered no precise numerical measure of temperature.

Later, scientists turned to tree rings, whose annual width reflects growing conditions in a given year. [■] This method extended the record back several thousand years, but it remained limited to forested zones and was easily disturbed by drought or disease. [■] Such gaps left long stretches of the deep past entirely unmeasured, frustrating attempts to chart ancient climate.

The modern breakthrough came with ice cores drilled from polar sheets, where trapped air bubbles store samples of ancient atmospheres. [■] By analyzing the chemistry of these bubbles, researchers can estimate temperatures stretching back hundreds of thousands of years. However, even ice cores face objections, since drilling sites are scarce and the deepest layers are often compressed beyond clear reading.

questions.stem: The word "patchy" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: incomplete
options.B: colorful
options.C: ancient
options.D: official

questions.explanation: The text says the written sources were "patchy, covering only a few centuries and a handful of regions," showing the records were incomplete in coverage.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what limited the usefulness of tree-ring records?

options.A: They could not be dated reliably to any one specific calendar year.
options.B: They directly contradicted the surviving written human harvest accounts.
options.C: They were confined to forested areas and disrupted by drought or disease.
options.D: They reached back only a few centuries into the recorded past.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the method "remained limited to forested zones and was easily disturbed by drought or disease."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about ice cores?

options.A: They have entirely replaced tree rings as the only useful climate record.
options.B: They provide detailed written descriptions of ancient weather events.
options.C: They can be drilled at almost any chosen location across the Earth.
options.D: They reach much further back in time than the earlier proxies described.

questions.explanation: Ice cores estimate temperatures "stretching back hundreds of thousands of years," far exceeding the few centuries of written accounts and few thousand years of tree rings.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention "harvest dates and reports of frozen rivers" in paragraph 1?

options.A: To prove that early climates were colder than today
options.B: To give concrete examples of the early written records used to reconstruct climate
options.C: To argue that written accounts are more reliable than ice cores
options.D: To explain how modern instruments measure temperature

questions.explanation: These items are offered as illustrations of the "written human accounts" that early efforts relied on to reconstruct past climate.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'And because such written entries were kept mainly for farming or trade, they recorded weather only when it disrupted daily life.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on the "written human accounts" just introduced and explains why they were "patchy," so it fits at the first marker, before that limitation is stated.

items.passage:
Sound synchronization is the technical alignment of recorded dialogue with the moving images of a film. In the silent era, studios faced a stubborn problem: spoken performances could not be captured alongside the picture, leaving stories to be carried by gesture and printed title cards. [■] Audiences sensed the absence of a human voice, and ambitious narratives strained against the limits of pantomime. The gap between what actors performed and what viewers could hear seemed unbridgeable.

The solution arrived through the sound-on-film process, which printed an optical audio track directly beside the picture frames. [■] Because both were exposed on a single strip, the voice and the image advanced together through the projector, locked in step. [■] This innovation freed directors to stage long passages of natural conversation, transforming the rhythm of storytelling almost overnight.

The outcomes reshaped the industry, yet not every consequence was welcome. [■] Performers prized for expressive faces sometimes possessed voices unsuited to the new medium, and careers collapsed. Studios also had to rebuild theaters at great expense. However, the demand for vocal talent grew so quickly that the transition, despite its costs, proved impossible to reverse.
paragraphs: Sound synchronization is the technical alignment of recorded dialogue with the moving images of a film. In the silent era, studios faced a stubborn problem: spoken performances could not be captured alongside the picture, leaving stories to be carried by gesture and printed title cards. [■] Audiences sensed the absence of a human voice, and ambitious narratives strained against the limits of pantomime. The gap between what actors performed and what viewers could hear seemed unbridgeable.

The solution arrived through the sound-on-film process, which printed an optical audio track directly beside the picture frames. [■] Because both were exposed on a single strip, the voice and the image advanced together through the projector, locked in step. [■] This innovation freed directors to stage long passages of natural conversation, transforming the rhythm of storytelling almost overnight.

The outcomes reshaped the industry, yet not every consequence was welcome. [■] Performers prized for expressive faces sometimes possessed voices unsuited to the new medium, and careers collapsed. Studios also had to rebuild theaters at great expense. However, the demand for vocal talent grew so quickly that the transition, despite its costs, proved impossible to reverse.

questions.stem: The word "stubborn" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: famous
options.B: ancient
options.C: persistent
options.D: minor

questions.explanation: The problem is described as one studios "faced" and that left storytelling strained, indicating a persistent, hard-to-solve difficulty.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how did the sound-on-film process keep voice and image aligned?

options.A: By recording the dialogue after the picture was filmed
options.B: By printing the audio track on the same strip as the picture frames
options.C: By training actors to match their speech to title cards
options.D: By slowing the projector to the speed of the spoken voice

questions.explanation: The passage says the process "printed an optical audio track directly beside the picture frames" so both "advanced together through the projector, locked in step."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following resulted from sound synchronization EXCEPT

options.A: Some actors lost their careers because of their voices.
options.B: Directors could stage long stretches of natural conversation.
options.C: Studios had to spend heavily to rebuild theaters.
options.D: Title cards became longer and more detailed than before.

questions.explanation: The passage credits sound with freeing directors, ending careers, and forcing theater rebuilds, but never says title cards grew longer; sound replaced reliance on them.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about silent-era storytelling?

options.A: It depended heavily on visual performance to convey meaning.
options.B: It was generally preferred by audiences over later films.
options.C: It used recorded dialogue printed on a separate strip.
options.D: It allowed directors to stage long spoken conversations.

questions.explanation: Because stories were "carried by gesture and printed title cards" and strained "against the limits of pantomime," silent storytelling leaned on visual performance.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, a comic star celebrated for graceful movements might be discarded once a thin or harsh voice reached the microphone.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The example concretely illustrates how "performers prized for expressive faces" with unsuitable voices saw "careers collapsed," so it belongs at the fourth marker, just before that sentence.

items.passage:
Entropy is a measure of how many microscopic arrangements correspond to the same observable state of a system. In thermodynamics, it is often described as a gauge of disorder, since states with many possible arrangements appear more mixed and less organized. [■] A neat stack of coins, for instance, can be arranged in few ways, while a scattered pile can occur in countless ways. The scattered pile therefore carries higher entropy than the orderly stack.

This quantity governs the direction of natural change. [■] When two objects at different temperatures touch, heat flows from the warmer to the cooler one, and the combined entropy of the pair rises until temperatures equalize. [■] The second law of thermodynamics captures this tendency, stating that the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases over time.

The significance of entropy reaches far beyond heat engines. [■] It explains why certain reactions proceed spontaneously and why perfectly efficient machines are impossible, since some energy always disperses as unusable heat. However, the concept faces objections when applied to the universe as a whole, where defining an isolated system and its boundaries remains deeply uncertain.
paragraphs: Entropy is a measure of how many microscopic arrangements correspond to the same observable state of a system. In thermodynamics, it is often described as a gauge of disorder, since states with many possible arrangements appear more mixed and less organized. [■] A neat stack of coins, for instance, can be arranged in few ways, while a scattered pile can occur in countless ways. The scattered pile therefore carries higher entropy than the orderly stack.

This quantity governs the direction of natural change. [■] When two objects at different temperatures touch, heat flows from the warmer to the cooler one, and the combined entropy of the pair rises until temperatures equalize. [■] The second law of thermodynamics captures this tendency, stating that the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases over time.

The significance of entropy reaches far beyond heat engines. [■] It explains why certain reactions proceed spontaneously and why perfectly efficient machines are impossible, since some energy always disperses as unusable heat. However, the concept faces objections when applied to the universe as a whole, where defining an isolated system and its boundaries remains deeply uncertain.

questions.stem: The word "gauge" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: boundary
options.B: engine
options.C: measure
options.D: container

questions.explanation: Entropy is introduced as "a measure of how many microscopic arrangements" exist, so "a gauge of disorder" means a measure of disorder.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens when two objects at different temperatures touch?

options.A: Heat moves from the warmer object to the cooler one and total entropy rises.
options.B: Heat moves from the cooler object to the warmer one until both objects freeze solid.
options.C: The combined entropy of the two objects steadily decreases.
options.D: The temperatures stay fixed because entropy cannot change.

questions.explanation: The passage states "heat flows from the warmer to the cooler one, and the combined entropy of the pair rises until temperatures equalize."

questions.stem: What does "this tendency" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the touching of two objects
options.B: the rise of total entropy over time
options.C: the equalizing of temperatures
options.D: the flow of heat into engines

questions.explanation: "This tendency" points back to the described rise in combined entropy, which the second law restates as entropy that "never decreases over time."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a perfectly efficient machine?

options.A: It would somehow reverse the natural direction of all spontaneous change.
options.B: It would steadily lower the total entropy of the entire universe.
options.C: It has already been successfully built and used in ordinary heat engines.
options.D: It cannot exist because some energy always escapes as unusable heat.

questions.explanation: The passage says perfectly efficient machines are "impossible, since some energy always disperses as unusable heat," implying such loss prevents perfect efficiency.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'And because efficiency depends on harnessing all available energy, that escaping heat sets a strict ceiling no design can exceed.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on the claim that perfect efficiency is impossible because energy "disperses as unusable heat," so it best follows that point at the fourth marker.

items.passage:
Domestication is the gradual process by which humans reshaped wild plants and animals to suit their own needs. The earliest stage involved simple gathering, as foraging communities harvested wild grasses and stored their seeds without planting them deliberately. These groups began to favor plants with larger or more reliable yields, and useful seeds were carried from place to place. Over generations, this casual selection slowly altered the plants they depended upon.

This early tinkering gave way to a more deliberate but still flawed stage of cultivation. Communities cleared small plots and sowed seeds, yet their crops remained close to wild forms, shattering easily and ripening unevenly. Harvests were unpredictable, and farmers often returned to gathering when fields failed. The knowledge needed to manage soil and water was only beginning to accumulate.

The decisive breakthrough came when sustained selection produced crops that depended on humans to reproduce, such as wheat whose seeds no longer scattered on their own. These fully domesticated plants yielded dependable surpluses that could feed settled villages. However, this achievement carried hidden costs, for reliance on a few crops left early farming societies vulnerable to drought and disease.
paragraphs: Domestication is the gradual process by which humans reshaped wild plants and animals to suit their own needs. The earliest stage involved simple gathering, as foraging communities harvested wild grasses and stored their seeds without planting them deliberately. These groups began to favor plants with larger or more reliable yields, and useful seeds were carried from place to place. Over generations, this casual selection slowly altered the plants they depended upon.

This early tinkering gave way to a more deliberate but still flawed stage of cultivation. Communities cleared small plots and sowed seeds, yet their crops remained close to wild forms, shattering easily and ripening unevenly. Harvests were unpredictable, and farmers often returned to gathering when fields failed. The knowledge needed to manage soil and water was only beginning to accumulate.

The decisive breakthrough came when sustained selection produced crops that depended on humans to reproduce, such as wheat whose seeds no longer scattered on their own. These fully domesticated plants yielded dependable surpluses that could feed settled villages. However, this achievement carried hidden costs, for reliance on a few crops left early farming societies vulnerable to drought and disease.

questions.stem: The word "favor" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: plant
options.B: prefer
options.C: discard
options.D: describe

questions.explanation: The communities "began to favor plants with larger or more reliable yields," meaning they preferred and selected such plants.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what was a problem with crops at the cultivation stage?

options.A: They could no longer reproduce without human help.
options.B: They produced surpluses too large for villages to store.
options.C: They required no management of soil or water.
options.D: They shattered easily and ripened unevenly.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the crops "remained close to wild forms, shattering easily and ripening unevenly."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about fully domesticated wheat?

options.A: It produced smaller harvests than wild grasses.
options.B: It spread its seeds more effectively than wild plants.
options.C: It allowed farmers to abandon settled village life.
options.D: It could not survive without human cultivation.

questions.explanation: Because domesticated wheat's "seeds no longer scattered on their own" and the crops "depended on humans to reproduce," such plants needed people to survive.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention that farmers "often returned to gathering when fields failed" in paragraph 2?

options.A: To emphasize how unreliable early cultivation still was
options.B: To prove that gathering produced larger yields than farming
options.C: To show that domestication had already been completed
options.D: To explain how villages first became permanent settlements

questions.explanation: The detail follows the statement that "harvests were unpredictable," illustrating how flawed and unreliable this intermediate stage of cultivation remained.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of domestication EXCEPT

options.A: Early foragers stored wild seeds without deliberately planting them.
options.B: Cultivated crops at the second stage stayed close to wild forms.
options.C: Dependence on domesticated crops removed the risk of drought and disease.
options.D: Fully domesticated crops yielded surpluses that fed settled villages.

questions.explanation: The passage says reliance on a few crops "left early farming societies vulnerable to drought and disease," the opposite of removing that risk.

items.passage:
Synthetic fibers are textile threads manufactured from chemical compounds rather than drawn from plants or animals. The earliest stage of fiber use depended entirely on natural materials, as weavers spun cotton, wool, and silk into cloth. These fibers were comfortable and widely available, yet their supply rose and fell with harvests and herds. Cloth makers had no way to control the length, strength, or smoothness of the threads nature provided.

This dependence prompted an intermediate stage of partly artificial fibers. Chemists dissolved natural cellulose from wood pulp and forced it through fine holes to form regenerated threads such as rayon. The result was cheaper and more uniform than silk, but the early versions weakened badly when wet and tore under modest strain. Manufacturers valued the lower cost while still struggling with poor durability.

The modern breakthrough arrived with fully synthetic fibers built from petroleum-based molecules, most famously nylon. These threads were engineered for exceptional strength, elasticity, and resistance to water. Their reliability made them essential for ropes, parachutes, and everyday clothing. However, this success brought new concerns, since such fibers resist natural decay and may contribute to long-lasting environmental waste.
paragraphs: Synthetic fibers are textile threads manufactured from chemical compounds rather than drawn from plants or animals. The earliest stage of fiber use depended entirely on natural materials, as weavers spun cotton, wool, and silk into cloth. These fibers were comfortable and widely available, yet their supply rose and fell with harvests and herds. Cloth makers had no way to control the length, strength, or smoothness of the threads nature provided.

This dependence prompted an intermediate stage of partly artificial fibers. Chemists dissolved natural cellulose from wood pulp and forced it through fine holes to form regenerated threads such as rayon. The result was cheaper and more uniform than silk, but the early versions weakened badly when wet and tore under modest strain. Manufacturers valued the lower cost while still struggling with poor durability.

The modern breakthrough arrived with fully synthetic fibers built from petroleum-based molecules, most famously nylon. These threads were engineered for exceptional strength, elasticity, and resistance to water. Their reliability made them essential for ropes, parachutes, and everyday clothing. However, this success brought new concerns, since such fibers resist natural decay and may contribute to long-lasting environmental waste.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The reasons cotton and wool still remain far more comfortable than nylon
options.B: The detailed chemical process used to dissolve raw cellulose from wood pulp
options.C: The development of textile fibers from natural to fully synthetic materials
options.D: The lasting environmental damage caused by petroleum-based modern clothing

questions.explanation: The passage traces fibers across three stages, from natural materials through regenerated rayon to fully synthetic nylon, making this overall development its focus.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how were early regenerated fibers such as rayon made?

options.A: By spinning together strands of cotton, wool, and harvested silk threads
options.B: By dissolving natural cellulose from wood pulp and forcing it through fine holes
options.C: By building long molecules directly from refined petroleum-based compounds
options.D: By weaving raw plant fibers together without any chemical treatment at all

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states chemists "dissolved natural cellulose from wood pulp and forced it through fine holes to form regenerated threads such as rayon."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It presents an intermediate stage that responded to the limitations described in paragraph 1.
options.B: It rejects and overturns the central claims about natural fibers made earlier in paragraph 1.
options.C: It simply repeats the very same information about natural fibers using slightly different words.
options.D: It fully describes the final petroleum-based synthetic fibers that were introduced in paragraph 1.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 opens with "This dependence prompted an intermediate stage," directly building on paragraph 1's account of uncontrollable natural fibers.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about nylon compared with rayon?

options.A: Nylon was made from the same wood pulp as rayon.
options.B: Nylon was more comfortable than cotton and wool.
options.C: Nylon was the first fiber ever used by weavers.
options.D: Nylon was more durable and water-resistant than the earlier regenerated fibers.

questions.explanation: Rayon "weakened badly when wet," while nylon offered "exceptional strength" and "resistance to water," implying nylon was far more durable and water-resistant.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what new concern accompanied fully synthetic fibers?

options.A: They resist natural decay and create long-lasting waste.
options.B: They were weaker than natural fibers when wet.
options.C: They could not be used for ropes or parachutes.
options.D: They depended on unreliable harvests and herds.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 notes that such fibers "resist natural decay and contribute to long-lasting environmental waste."
